case_metadata.user_request: Find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for the adsorbate with SMILES [N]=N on the provided surface.
case_metadata.notes: OCD id=118; adsorbate=[N]=N; surface_formula=Ge36V60; y_relaxed=-2.727881 eV; site=37;47;53;62;65;86

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N, i.e. diazenyl/dinitrogen-derived species with two N atoms. The provided atom list must be followed strictly: atom 0 is N, SP2, degree 1, radical_electrons 1; atom 1 is N, SP2, degree 1, radical_electrons 0. There is no mismatch between the SMILES and the intended adsorbate. Because I plan to bind through atom 0 if using ReactiveSpecies logic, the radical requirement is satisfied since atom 0 has one unpaired electron. History analysis: two plans were already tested. (1) Planned ontop on V slipped to a hollow 5-fold V-V-V-V site and gave E = -10.138 eV; this explicitly marks ontop V as unstable and V-type sites should be forbidden for future planning. (2) Planned bridge Ge-V slipped to a hollow Ge-V-V-V environment and gave E = -9.624 eV; this explicitly marks Ge-V bridge as unstable and Ge-V type sites should also be forbidden. The best known energy so far is the slipped hollow V-rich state at -10.138 eV. No convergence-to-known-best termination tag is present, so I should still propose one new chemically distinct plan. User intent: find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for [N]=N on the Ge/V surface by systematically exploring sites. Adsorbate analysis: [N]=N can adsorb end-on through one N center or side-on through both N atoms. Because this SMILES includes a radical on atom 0, ReactiveSpecies classification is appropriate if atom 0 is used as the binding atom. Surface analysis: available untested canonical options that avoid forbidden site chemistries include ontop Ge, bridge Ge-Ge, bridge V-V, hollow Ge-Ge-V, hollow Ge-V-V, hollow V-V-V, hollow Ge-Ge-Ge-V, hollow Ge-Ge-V-V, and the 5-fold Ge-V-V-V-V representation under canonical hollow. Since the best observed relaxed structure was a V-rich hollow and V ontop/Ge-V bridge both spontaneously migrated toward hollow, the most physically motivated next test is a direct hollow placement in a V-rich environment rather than retrying forbidden unstable initial sites. To respect the site restrictions and remain distinct from prior plans, I will test a hollow V-V-V site. This directly probes whether N2-like species prefers a 3-fold V-rich hollow without relying on unstable V ontop or Ge-V bridge starts. I use a single binding index [0] because only atom 0 is radical-bearing and this keeps the plan chemically valid for ReactiveSpecies while allowing the molecule to relax into end-on or tilted adsorption at the hollow ensemble.

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the provided adsorbate is [N]=N. The atom list shows two N atoms with indices 0 and 1, both SP2-hybridized; atom 0 has 1 radical electron and atom 1 has 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between the SMILES and the intended adsorbate description because the user only requested the species defined by the SMILES. Because I plan to use the radical-containing N atom as the binding center, ReactiveSpecies is chemically justified and the chosen binding atom does have unpaired electrons. History analysis: there are no previous attempts, so no known stable or unstable motifs yet, and no duplicate/converged cases. User intent: find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for N2-like radical species on the Ge/V surface. Adsorbate analysis: [N]=N is a diatomic unsaturated species; likely favorable motifs on transition-metal-containing surfaces include end-on coordination through one N atom, especially at a V site, and possibly side-on bridging in later attempts if needed. Surface analysis: available canonical site families are ontop, bridge, and hollow, with actual local chemistries including Ge and V. Since V is generally more chemically active than Ge for N-binding, the best initial low-energy hypothesis is end-on adsorption on an ontop V site through the radical N atom (index 0). This is also the most conservative physically consistent first trial before exploring bridge or hollow motifs. I will therefore start with ontop adsorption on V using adsorbate binding index [0].

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N, i.e. diazenyl-like N2 with heavy-atom indices 0 and 1. RDKit reports atom 0 as N, SP2, degree 1, radical_electrons 1; atom 1 as N, SP2, degree 1, radical_electrons 0. There is no user-description/SMILES mismatch to warn about. Because one selected binding atom can legitimately be the radical center, ReactiveSpecies is chemically appropriate if binding through atom 0. History analysis: only one successful attempt exists, planned as ontop on V with adsorbate index [0], but it slipped to a hollow V-V-V-V environment and explicitly triggered an unstable-site warning forbidding V-type sites again. Therefore ontop V is invalid/unstable and any new plan involving V ontop should be avoided. The slipped product at a V-rich hollow gave E = -10.138 eV and is current best. No convergence-to-known-best tag is present, so termination is not yet justified. User intent is to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration of [N]=N on this Ge/V surface. Adsorbate analysis: N=N can adsorb end-on through one N or side-on through both N atoms; since atom 0 has the unpaired electron, testing atom-0-led adsorption is especially reasonable. Surface analysis: available canonical site classes are ontop, bridge, hollow, with chemistries Ge-Ge, Ge-V, V-V bridges; Ge-Ge-V, Ge-V-V, V-V-V, Ge-Ge-Ge-V, Ge-Ge-V-V, Ge-V-V-V-V hollows; ontop Ge and V. Since V sites are forbidden by history, the most systematic unexplored alternative is a Ge-containing site. To maximize adsorption strength while respecting the warning, a mixed Ge-V bridge is a good next test because it probes cooperative alloy binding without being a pure V-type ontop retry. Using one adsorbate binding index keeps alignment valid for a bridge end-on motif and avoids overcommitting to a side-on geometry that may simply relax back to the known V-rich hollow. Thus I will test end-on adsorption through radical N atom 0 at a Ge-V bridge.

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N, i.e. diazenyl-like N2 with heavy atom indices 0 and 1 provided explicitly. RDKit reports atom 0 has 1 radical electron and atom 1 has 0 radical electrons, so if using ReactiveSpecies the binding must involve atom 0. Hybridization is SP2 on both N atoms; there is no mismatch with the user request because the request is simply to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for this SMILES. History analysis: three plans were tested. (1) ontop on V using one binding atom slipped away; V ontop is explicitly marked unstable and must be forbidden. Energy after slip: -10.138 eV. (2) bridge on Ge-V slipped; Ge-V bridge is explicitly unstable and must be forbidden. Energy: -9.624 eV. (3) hollow on V-V-V slipped to an even more coordinated V-rich hollow and is currently best at -11.384 eV; the planned V-V-V hollow is explicitly unstable and must be forbidden. There is no [🔄 Converged to known best] tag, so termination is not yet mandatory. User intent is to identify the lowest-energy adsorption mode, so the next plan should target an untested, physically plausible high-coordination environment while respecting forbidden site types. Surface analysis: available untested site chemistries include bridge Ge-Ge and V-V; ontop Ge; hollows Ge-Ge-V, Ge-V-V, Ge-Ge-Ge-V, Ge-Ge-V-V, and 5-fold Ge-V-V-V-V, though JSON site_type must remain one of ontop/bridge/hollow. Since previous unstable trials all relaxed toward more coordinated V-rich hollows and the best state is a V-rich hollow, a still-untested mixed hollow with strong V participation is the most promising next systematic choice. To avoid forbidden V-V-V and Ge-V planned motifs, choose a distinct hollow composition Ge-Ge-V-V, encoded canonically as hollow. Because [N]=N can adsorb side-on and only atom 0 is radical-active, the chemically safe choice under ReactiveSpecies rules is end-on binding through atom 0 only, letting relaxation discover whether stronger multi-center interaction develops. This is a completely new plan and systematically probes an alloy hollow environment not yet tested.

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=N, i.e. dinitrogen in the provided representation. The RDKit atom list shows two N atoms with indices 0 and 1, both SP2-hybridized in this parser output; there is no user-description mismatch to resolve. Atom 0 has 1 radical electron while atom 1 has 0, so if choosing ReactiveSpecies, only atom 0 is formally radical-capable. However, for intact N2 adsorption on a surface, treating it as a molecular adsorbate is still chemically reasonable because end-on donation from N lone-pair character is plausible without requiring radical binding. History analysis: tested plans were ontop V, bridge Ge-V, hollow V-V-V, and hollow Ge-Ge-V-V. All four were reported as unstable and slipped away from the intended initial site. Therefore V ontop, Ge-V bridge, V-V-V hollow, and Ge-Ge-V-V hollow are invalid/unstable and should not be retried. The best energy found so far is -11.384 eV from a slipped configuration reached from V-V-V hollow, indicating hollow environments rich in V are favorable thermodynamically, but the originally planned V-V-V site itself is unstable. Since we must propose a completely new plan and should avoid forbidden site chemistries, remaining untested high-symmetry options include ontop Ge, bridge Ge-Ge, bridge V-V, hollow Ge-Ge-V, hollow Ge-V-V, hollow Ge-Ge-Ge-V, and hollow Ge-V-V-V-V. Because V-only related motifs have already shown instability as initial guesses, and Ge-V mixed bridge is poor, the most systematic new chemically distinct trial is a Ge-containing hollow that still retains some V participation: Ge-Ge-V. For N2, a side-on or di-sigma style placement over a 3-fold hollow can be explored using both N atoms as binding indices. This is different from all previous plans, respects available site types, and probes alloy heterogeneity without reusing forbidden initial site types. User intent is to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration, so the next plan should target an untested, plausible competitive site rather than terminate.
